source_metadata.title_raw: Ryan R. Jones, Esq. - I-18-148 - August 6, 2018 - Fullerton

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 6, 2018

Ryan R. Jones, Esq.
Jones & Mayer, PLC
3777 North Harbor Blvd.
Fullerton, CA 92835

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-18-148

Dear Mr. Jones:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of ("Act")¹ on behalf of City of Bishop Councilmember Chris Costello. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interests prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Since your advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision but seeks general guidance, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Does City of Bishop Councilmember Chris Costello have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act if he volunteers with the Bishop Fire Department?

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Costello from becoming a volunteer firefighter with the Bishop Fire Department. The Act's conflict of interest provisions only prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in governmental decisions in which he has a financial interest. Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Costello may be disqualified from City Council

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-18-148
Page No. 2

decisions implicating the Bishop Fire Department only to the extent that the decisions will
foreseeably and materially affect his personal finances.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

Councilmember Costello wishes to serve as a volunteer firefighter with the Bishop Fire
Department, as he did prior to becoming a member on the City Council. Volunteer firefighters only
respond to calls as needed and are paid $20 per response. In a given month, a volunteer might make
$100-$200.

The Fire Department relies almost exclusively on volunteers, with the Fire Chief being a
salaried employee of the City. Bishop has a city administrator style of government. Therefore, the
City Council selects and evaluates the Fire Chief.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section.

Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. Most pertinent
to the analysis are the following:

• An interest in any source of income to the official aggregating $500 or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to, the public official within 12 months prior to the
decision.
• An interest in a business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family, also known as the "personal financial effects" rule.

You stated that Councilmember Costello intends on becoming a volunteer firefighter again
with the Bishop Fire Department. As a volunteer firefighter, it is likely that the Councilmember will
receive more than $500 from the Department in the 12 months prior to any Fire Department-related
decision coming before the City Council.

However, under Section 82030(b)(2) salary, reimbursement for expenses, and per diem from
a governmental agency are not considered income for purposes of the Act. Additionally,
government agencies are not organizations or enterprises operated for profit. Therefore,
Councilmember Costello does not have an interest in the Bishop Fire Department as either a source
of income or a business entity.

As a volunteer firefighter, Councilmember Costello will be disqualified from decisions
implicating the Bishop Fire Department only to the extent that the decisions affect his interest in his
personal finances. Accordingly, we note that while a decision affecting governmental salary is not

File No. I-18-148
Page No. 3

generally disqualifying, an official may be disqualified from a decision based upon an effect on the
official's personal finances if the decision is a decision to appoint, hire, fire, promote, demote, or
suspend without pay or take a disciplinary action with financial sanction against the official or his
or her immediate family, or set salary for the official or his or her immediate family different from
salaries paid to other employees of the agency in the same job classification or position. (See
Regulation 8704(c)(3).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgt
